content_markdown:
A standing (stationary) wave is set up on a stretched string. The diagram below shows the string at three different instants of time. P, Q and R are three points on the string.

Which of the following gives two points on the string that are in phase and two points on the string that are one wavelength apart?

diagram_metadata.description: A standing wave diagram showing three loops. Point P is at the center of the first loop, point Q is at the center of the second loop, and point R is at the center of the third loop. The string is shown in its maximum positive displacement, maximum negative displacement, and equilibrium positions.

options.label: A
options.text: In phase: P and Q; One wavelength apart: P and R

options.label: B
options.text: In phase: P and R; One wavelength apart: P and R

options.label: C
options.text: In phase: P and Q; One wavelength apart: P and Q

options.label: D
options.text: In phase: P and R; One wavelength apart: P and Q

vibe_explanation: In a standing wave, all points within a single loop (between two adjacent nodes) are in phase with each other, while points in adjacent loops are $180^\circ$ out of phase. Therefore, P and R (separated by two loops) are in phase, while P and Q (separated by one loop) are out of phase. One wavelength $\lambda$ in a standing wave corresponds to the length of two loops; thus, the distance from the center of the first loop (P) to the center of the third loop (R) is exactly one wavelength.
